headline: Cyber Stars and Oil's Surge Set the Tone as Semis Nurse Tuesday's Bruises
body_html:
Markets head into Wednesday's open carrying a split inheritance from Tuesday's session: a sharp cybersecurity rally that lifted the Software sector 0.81% and sent several security-focused names surging by double digits, set against a semiconductor complex that shed 3.43% — the worst sectoral performance of the day — dragging 213 names lower across our 396-name universe versus only 180 advancers. Traders will watch whether that divergence hardens or reverses as pre-market positioning unfolds.

The geopolitical backdrop provides Wednesday's most pressing catalyst. Crude climbed roughly 2% overnight after an escalation of hostilities in the Middle East, with Iran threatening to restrict additional key waterways and Iran-linked tankers moving through the Strait of Hormuz ahead of a renewed U.S. blockade order. Energy finished Tuesday as the session's only meaningful winner, up 1.96%, and early indications suggest that bid has legs. Any fresh Hormuz-related headlines before the bell could reprice energy equities quickly.

On the policy front, a U.S.-Russia sanctions bill is being watched for its potential to ease trade friction with China and India, while Senate Democrats' move to stall a large defense-spending measure adds another layer of uncertainty. IBM's earnings-related commentary on enterprise security demand appears to be the proximate trigger for Tuesday's cybersecurity surge; watch whether that momentum carries into the broader software tape at the open.

briefs.headline: Cybersecurity Names Roar After IBM Signals Enterprise Security Strength
briefs.body: Tuesday's standout story was a broad-based cybersecurity rally apparently ignited by IBM commentary on robust enterprise security demand. CrowdStrike surged 12.14%, Okta gained 10.81%, Qualys added 8.30%, Zscaler rose 7.24%, and Palo Alto Networks climbed 6.84% — a synchronized move rarely seen across multiple large-cap security names in a single session.

briefs.headline: Hormuz Flashpoint Sends Oil Up 2% Overnight
briefs.body: Crude prices rose approximately 2% as Middle East tensions escalated sharply, with Iran threatening to restrict additional vital sea lanes and Iran-linked vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz ahead of a U.S.-ordered blockade. Energy was Tuesday's sole winning sector at plus 1.96%, and the overnight bid suggests the trade could extend into Wednesday's open.

briefs.headline: Semiconductors Bear the Week's Heaviest Losses
briefs.body: The semiconductor sector dropped 3.43% on Tuesday — the steepest sectoral decline in our universe. Rambus fell 8.02%, Marvell Technology lost 7.75%, Alpha and Omega Semiconductor slid 7.64%, Vicor dropped 7.31%, Amkor Technology declined 6.26%, and Entegris shed 6.25%, painting a broad picture of sector-wide selling pressure heading into Wednesday.

briefs.headline: Senate Democrats Halt Massive Defense Spending Bill Over Iran War Concerns
briefs.body: Democratic senators moved to block a roughly $1.15 trillion defense authorization measure, citing objections to provisions they believe could facilitate military action against Iran. The vote adds fresh uncertainty to both defense-sector budget visibility and the trajectory of U.S.-Iran relations, two threads markets are already closely tracking this week.

briefs.headline: U.S.-Russia Sanctions Legislation May Ease China and India Trade Pressure
briefs.body: A bill targeting Russian sanctions is drawing attention for a secondary effect: analysts suggest it could reduce the likelihood of tariff escalation against China and India. If the legislation advances, it may offer modest relief for multinationals with significant exposure to either market, a dynamic worth monitoring as the measure moves through Congress.

briefs.headline: Lebanon-Israel Talks Resume in Rome Under U.S. Mediation
briefs.body: Lebanese and Israeli delegations met in Rome under U.S. auspices to advance implementation of an existing framework agreement. Progress, if sustained, could modestly reduce the geopolitical risk premium embedded in energy markets — though with Hormuz tensions simultaneously escalating, any calming effect from the Rome talks may be offset in crude pricing.
